claim: Rats and mice are dichromats who possess ultraviolet and green cones, but not red cones.
source: These animals are assumed to be functionally blind under red light, an assumption that might originate in the fact that they are dichromats who possess ultraviolet and green cones, but not red cones.
evidence: This is supported by established scientific knowledge about rodent photoreceptor types, as referenced in the manuscript's citations.

claim: Long-Evans rats can perform visual form discrimination under red light.
source: Psychometric curves plotting judged orientation versus angle demonstrate the conserved visual capacity of rats under red light.
evidence: This claim is supported by the experimental data presented in the psychometric curves showing performance under red light illumination.

claim: Rats should not be assumed to be blind under red light in investigations exploring rodent physiological and behavioral functions.
source: Investigations aiming to explore rodent physiological and behavioral functions in the absence of visual input should not assume redlight blindness.
evidence: This is a conclusion drawn from the experimental findings presented in the study.

claim: Rats are largely crepuscular and usually found in poorly illuminated environments during daylight.
source: Rats, like many rodents, are largely crepuscular and, even during daylight, are usually to be found in poorly illuminated environments (Macdonald et al., 1994).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Macdonald et al., 1994.

claim: Rat retina is rod dominated, with cones making up as little as 1% of photoreceptors.
source: Their retina is rod dominated, with cones making up as little as 1% of photoreceptors (Jacobs et al., 2001; La Vail, 1976).
evidence: This is supported by citations to Jacobs et al., 2001 and La Vail, 1976.

claim: Rods are about 100 times more sensitive than cones.
source: Rods are about 100 times more sensitive (Yau, 1994) and allow vision under low-light conditions, with a peak spectral absorption of rhodopsin in rods in rats and mice at 498 nm (Bridges, 1959; Govardovskii et al., 2000; Lyubarsky et al., 2004).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Yau, 1994.

claim: Peak spectral absorption of rhodopsin in rods in rats and mice is at 498 nm.
source: Rods are about 100 times more sensitive (Yau, 1994) and allow vision under low-light conditions, with a peak spectral absorption of rhodopsin in rods in rats and mice at 498 nm (Bridges, 1959; Govardovskii et al., 2000; Lyubarsky et al., 2004).
evidence: This is supported by citations to Bridges, 1959; Govardovskii et al., 2000; Lyubarsky et al., 2004.

claim: Rats are not color-blind.
source: Although rats are not color-blind (Jacobs et al., 2001; Lemmon and Anderson, 1979; Muenzinger and Reynolds, 1936; Munn and Collins, 1936; Walton and Bornemeier, 1938), they perform poorly in discriminating between nearby wavelengths, compared to humans (Walton, 1933).
evidence: This is supported by multiple citations: Jacobs et al., 2001; Lemmon and Anderson, 1979; Muenzinger and Reynolds, 1936; Munn and Collins, 1936; Walton and Bornemeier, 1938.

claim: Rats perform poorly in discriminating between nearby wavelengths compared to humans.
source: Although rats are not color-blind (Jacobs et al., 2001; Lemmon and Anderson, 1979; Muenzinger and Reynolds, 1936; Munn and Collins, 1936; Walton and Bornemeier, 1938), they perform poorly in discriminating between nearby wavelengths, compared to humans (Walton, 1933).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Walton, 1933.

claim: Rats' color sensitivity is based on two sets of cones with peak sensitivities in the range of ultraviolet (358–359 nm) and green (509–510 nm).
source: Rats' color sensitivity is based on two sets of cones with peak sensitivities in the range of ultraviolet (UV cones; peak absorption at 358–359 nm) and green (M cones; peak absorption at 509–510 nm) (Deegan and Jacobs, 1993; Jacobs et al., 1991; Sze´l and Ro¨hlich, 1992).
evidence: This is supported by citations to Deegan and Jacobs, 1993; Jacobs et al., 1991; Sze´l and Ro¨hlich, 1992.

claim: Electroretinogram measurements of Wistar rats identified two sensitivity peaks at 362 and 502 nm with no significant response to long wavelength light above 620 nm.
source: Recently, electroretinogram (ERG) responses of the photopic spectral sensitivity curves of photoreceptors of rats and mice were measured throughout the UV–visible spectrum (300–700 nm) (Rocha et al., 2016). These measurements identified two sensitivity peaks in Wistar rats: 362 and 502 nm; no significant response to long wavelength light (above 620 nm) was detected.
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Rocha et al., 2016.

claim: A study by Niklaus et al., 2020 showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.
source: In contrast, another study (Niklaus et al., 2020) showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Niklaus et al., 2020.

claim: Four Long-Evans rats were used in the study.
source: To quantify rats' performance, we used a cumulative Gaussian function to fit psychometric curves to the data of each rat (see Materials and methods). Figure 1E reveals that all rats (N = 4, pale curves; average curve in dark blue) performed well under white LED illumination.
evidence: This is directly stated in the methods and results sections.

claim: All four rats performed well under white LED illumination.
source: Figure 1E reveals that all rats (N = 4, pale curves; average curve in dark blue) performed well under white LED illumination.
evidence: This is supported by data shown in Figure 1E.

claim: Dark-adapted rats performed the visual categorization task under 626 nm and 652 nm LEDs with accuracy equivalent to that under white light.
source: Figure 2A, B shows that dark-adapted rats performed the visual categorization task under 626 nm and 652 nm LEDs (perceived as red by humans) with accuracy equivalent to that under white light, refuting the common belief of functional blindness under red light.
evidence: This is supported by data shown in Figure 2A and 2B.

claim: Rats performed well under peak 729 nm illumination, though clearly diminished with respect to white light.
source: Rats performed well even under peak 729 nm (Figure 2C), perceived as far-red by humans, though clearly diminished with respect to white.
evidence: This is supported by data shown in Figure 2C.

claim: Rats performed poorly under infrared illumination comprising 854 nm and 930 nm light.
source: They performed poorly under infrared illumination, comprising 854 nm and 930 nm light (Figure 2D,E).
evidence: This is supported by data shown in Figure 2D and 2E.

claim: Average performance was 87 ± 2% correct under white light.
source: Average performance was 87 `±` 2% correct under white light (confidence interval is SEM across rats).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data.

claim: Average performance was 84 ± 3% under 626 nm illumination with p=0.158 compared to white light.
source: Average performance was 84 `±` 3% under 626 nm illumination (p=0.158 compared to white light; bootstrap tests illustrated in Figure 2—figure supplement 1)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data with statistical testing.

claim: Average performance was 86 ± 2% under 652 nm illumination with p=0.419 compared to white light.
source: 86 `±` 2% under 652 nm (p=0.419 compared to white light)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data with statistical testing.

claim: Average performance was 72 ± 3% under 729 nm illumination with p=0.00 compared to white light.
source: 72 `±` 3% under 729 nm (p=0.00 compared to white light)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data with statistical testing.

claim: Average performance was 53 ± 2% under 854 nm illumination with p=0.257 compared to chance.
source: 53 `±` 2% under 854 nm (p=0.257 compared to chance)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data with statistical testing.

claim: Average performance was 50 ± 1% under 930 nm illumination with p=0.316 compared to chance.
source: 50 `±` 1% under 930 nm average performance (p=0.316 compared to chance).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data with statistical testing.

claim: Visual acuity under red LEDs (626 nm and 652 nm) cannot be explained by unintended 'leakage' toward shorter wavelengths.
source: Visual acuity under the red LEDs (626 nm and 652 nm) cannot be explained by unintended 'leakage' toward shorter wavelengths. Figure 1D shows that the infrared LEDs (854 nm and 930 nm) emitted `~` 2–50 times higher intensity at shorter wavelength than did the red LEDs, yet performance under illumination by IR LEDs was at chance.
evidence: This is an inference drawn from comparing the spectral data in Figure 1D with the behavioral performance data.

claim: Performance under red LEDs is better explained by positing that enough red light was absorbed to guide the discrimination.
source: Therefore, performance under red LEDs is better explained by positing that enough red light was absorbed to guide the discrimination.
evidence: This is an inference drawn from the experimental results.

claim: Rodent behavioral experiments done under red light are believed to place the animal in dark conditions while allowing the experimenter to observe the preparation.
source: Rodent behavioral experiments done under red light are believed to place the animal in dark conditions while allowing the experimenter to observe the preparation directly or by video recording (Celikel and Sakmann, 2007; Cloke et al., 2015; Diamond et al., 1999; Englund et al., 2020; van Goethem et al., 2012; Harris and Diamond, 2000; Harris et al., 1999; Jacklin et al., 2016; Nikbakht et al., 2012; Pacchiarini et al., 2020; Reid et al., 2014; Salaberry et al., 2017; Sieben et al., 2015; Vasconcelos et al., 2011; Winters and Reid, 2010).
evidence: This is supported by multiple citations documenting this common practice.

claim: Place-cell studies of rats navigating under dim red light have been conducted with the assumption of absence of visual input.
source: The relevant literature includes place-cell studies of rats navigating under dim red light, taken as the absence of visual input (O'Keefe, 1976; Save et al., 2000; Zhang and Manahan-Vaughan, 2015; Zhang et al., 2014).
evidence: This is supported by citations to studies that made this assumption.

claim: Red illumination is often used in reverse light cycle conditions in animal husbandry settings with the assumption that it will not affect rodent circadian rhythms.
source: Furthermore, red illumination is often used in reverse light cycle conditions in animal husbandry settings with the assumption that it will not affect rodent circadian rhythms (Emmer et al., 2018).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Emmer et al., 2018.

claim: Animals in studies using red light may acquire more visual input than previously believed.
source: In all of these cases, the animals may acquire more visual input than previously believed.
evidence: This is an inference drawn from the experimental findings of the current study.

claim: Rodents lack red cones.
source: Rodents lack red cones (Deegan and Jacobs, 1993; Jacobs et al., 1991; Sze´l and Ro¨hlich, 1992), but from the inability to see red as a color, it does not necessarily follow that they cannot absorb red light through their rod-dominated retina to support form vision.
evidence: This is supported by citations to Deegan and Jacobs, 1993; Jacobs et al., 1991; Sze´l and Ro¨hlich, 1992.

claim: The inability to see red as a color does not necessarily mean rodents cannot absorb red light through their rod-dominated retina to support form vision.
source: Rodents lack red cones (Deegan and Jacobs, 1993; Jacobs et al., 1991; Sze´l and Ro¨hlich, 1992), but from the inability to see red as a color, it does not necessarily follow that they cannot absorb red light through their rod-dominated retina to support form vision.
evidence: This is a logical inference and interpretation of the experimental findings.

claim: Both Brown Norway and Wistar rat strains showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light (656 ± 10 nm) even at low intensities.
source: A recent study (Niklaus et al., 2020) examined the retinal responses mediated by rods and cones of pigmented (Brown Norway) and albino (Wistar) rats in response to monochromatic far-red light of 656 `±` 10 nm in both photopic (light-adapted) and scotopic (dark-adapted) settings. Both rat strains showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Niklaus et al., 2020.

claim: Excitation of retinal receptors by red light does not, by itself, indicate behavioral availability of the signal.
source: Excitation of retinal receptors by red light does not, by itself, indicate behavioral availability of the signal.
evidence: This is a logical statement based on scientific reasoning.

claim: Red light might act to entrain circadian rhythms, unconscious to the animal.
source: For instance, red light might act to entrain circadian rhythms, unconscious to the animal.
evidence: This is a speculative statement about a possible mechanism.

claim: Red illumination delivered into the brain in an optogenetic protocol evoked a behavioral artifact in mice performing a visually guided discrimination task.
source: The sensitivity of mice is emphasized by the finding that red illumination delivered into the brain in an optogenetic protocol evoked a behavioral artifact in mice performing a visually guided discrimination task (Danskin et al., 2015).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Danskin et al., 2015.

claim: Mechanisms must exist for converting miniscule quantities of receptor activation into meaningful signals, perhaps through some cross-retina population code.
source: Although our study does not include direct measures of photoreceptor sensitivity, the results indicate that mechanisms must exist for converting miniscule quantities of receptor activation into meaningful signals, perhaps through some cross-retina population code.
evidence: This is an inference and speculation based on the experimental results.

claim: Photoreceptors could respond to longer wavelengths through a nonlinear optical process including two-photon activation of photopigments.
source: It is interesting to note the possibility that photoreceptors could respond to longer wavelengths through a nonlinear optical process including two-photon activation of photopigments (Palczewska et al., 2014; Vinberg et al., 2019), although the emission intensities used in our work are many orders of magnitude lower than those used in two-photon studies (Palczewska et al., 2014).
evidence: This is a speculative statement supported by citations to Palczewska et al., 2014 and Vinberg et al., 2019, but the authors note their intensities are too low for this mechanism.

claim: Object discrimination began to degrade when illumination wavelength increased from 652 to 729 nm and was almost nil at 854 nm.
source: Object discrimination began to degrade when illumination wavelength increased from 652 to 729 nm and was almost nil at 854 nm.
evidence: This is a summary of the experimental data.

claim: Illumination at 850 nm did not support visual form capacity, yet remains within the sensitivity range of inexpensive silicone detectors (CMOS and CCDs).
source: The performance curves suggest the optimal conditions for vision-excluded behavioral studies: illumination at 850 nm did not support visual form capacity, yet remains within the sensitivity range of inexpensive silicone detectors (CMOS and CCDs).
evidence: This is an inference from the experimental data combined with knowledge about detector sensitivity.

claim: Rats and mice are the most frequently used laboratory mammals.
source: Rats and mice are the most frequently used laboratory mammals, ideal for research on spatial navigation (Frank et al., 2000; MacDonald et al., 2011; Moser et al., 2017; O'keefe and Nadel, 1978; Pastalkova et al., 2008; Wood et al., 2000) and the processing of tactile (Diamond et al., 2008; Fassihi et al., 2017; Zuo and Diamond, 2019) and olfactory (Chae et al., 2019; Koldaeva et al., 2019; Uchida and Mainen, 2003; Uchida et al., 2006) information.
evidence: This is general knowledge supported by multiple citations.

claim: There is growing interest in the use of rodents for the study of vision, alone or combined with other modalities.
source: However, there is growing interest in the use of rodents for the study of vision, alone or combined with other modalities (Gharaei et al., 2018; Nikbakht et al., 2018; Nikbakht Nasrabadi, 2015; Sieben et al., 2015; Zoccolan, 2015).
evidence: This is supported by citations to recent studies.

claim: Rats under broad-wavelength conditions spontaneously recognize an object even when views differ by angle, size, and position.
source: Rats under broad-wavelength conditions spontaneously recognize an object even when views differ by angle, size, and position Zoccolan, 2015; such generalization is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Zoccolan, 2015.

claim: Object generalization across different views is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.
source: such generalization is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.
evidence: This is based on established knowledge in the field of visual perception.

claim: Rats achieve high-level sensory-perceptual cognition through the workings of neuronal circuits that are accessible.
source: Importantly, they achieve high-level sensory-perceptual cognition through the workings of neuronal circuits that are accessible (Crochet et al., 2019; Matteucci and Zoccolan, 2020; Matteucci et al., 2019; Steinmetz et al., 2019; Tafazoli et al., 2017).
evidence: This is supported by citations to multiple recent studies.

claim: Four male Long-Evans rats from Charles River Laboratories were used in the study.
source: Four male Long–Evans rats (Charles River Laboratories, Calco, Italy) were used.
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental subjects.

claim: Rats were caged in pairs and maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle.
source: They were caged in pairs and maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle; experiments were conducted during the light phase.
evidence: This is a direct statement about experimental conditions.

claim: Experiments were conducted during the light phase of the light/dark cycle.
source: They were caged in pairs and maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle; experiments were conducted during the light phase.
evidence: This is a direct statement about experimental conditions.

claim: Rats were 8 weeks old and weighed approximately 250 g upon arrival.
source: Upon arrival, they were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental subjects.

claim: Rats typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
source: Upon arrival, they were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental subjects.

claim: Rats received 17–22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward during each session.
source: During each session, they received 17–22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward.
evidence: This is a direct statement about experimental procedures.

claim: Rats were dark-adapted in a light-free environment for 20–30 min prior to each session.
source: Rats were dark-adapted in a light-free environment for 20–30 min prior to each session.
evidence: This is a direct statement about experimental procedures.

claim: The stimulus was a black and white square-wave grating within a circular 9.8 cm diameter circumference.
source: The stimulus was a black and white square-wave grating within a circular 9.8 cm diameter circumference, built in-house by a 3D printer (3D Touch, BFB Technologies, Figure 1A).
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental stimulus, with reference to Figure 1A.

claim: The spatial frequency of the gratings would be 0.075 cycles per degree of visual angle.
source: The spatial frequency of the gratings would be `1` = `ð2` tan `� 1ð3` : `5` = `30ÞÞ ¼ 0` : `075` cycles per degree of visual angle.
evidence: This is a calculated value based on the experimental setup geometry.

claim: The normal visual acuity of Long-Evans rats has been estimated as ~1 cpd.
source: As the normal visual acuity of Long– Evans rats has been estimated as `~` 1 cpd (Prusky et al., 2002), the bars would be expected to be resolvable.
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Prusky et al., 2002.

claim: The bars of the grating would be expected to be resolvable by Long-Evans rats.
source: As the normal visual acuity of Long– Evans rats has been estimated as `~` 1 cpd (Prusky et al., 2002), the bars would be expected to be resolvable.
evidence: This is an inference based on comparing the spatial frequency of the stimulus to the known visual acuity of the rats.

claim: Rats have a large depth of focus, from 7 cm to infinity.
source: Rats also have a large depth of focus, from 7 cm to infinity (Powers and Green, 1978).
evidence: This is supported by a citation to Powers and Green, 1978.

claim: The width of the binocular field directly in front of the rat's nose ranges from approximately 40°–110°, depending on head pitch.
source: The width of the binocular field directly in front of the rat's nose, generally considered the animal's binocular viewing area (Mei et al., 2012), ranges from approximately 40˚–110˚, depending on head pitch (Wallace et al., 2013).
evidence: This is supported by citations to Mei et al., 2012 and Wallace et al., 2013.

claim: The 117° stimulus should completely cover the rat's binocular visual field.
source: The 117˚ stimulus should thus completely cover the rat's binocular visual field.
evidence: This is an inference based on comparing the stimulus size to the known binocular field of rats.

claim: When all illumination sources were turned off, the ambient light magnitude was 0 cd/mm².
source: When all illumination sources were turned off, the ambient light magnitude was 0 cd/mm[2] (Konica Minolta LS-100 luminance meter, Tokyo).
evidence: This is a direct measurement of the experimental conditions.

claim: Duration of training to reach stable performance was typically 4–6 weeks, with one session per day.
source: Duration of training to reach stable performance was typically 4–6 weeks, with one session per day, and varied according to individual differences in rate of learning.
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental procedures.

claim: The sequence of sessions for each rat was randomized, with each day's session illuminated by either red, infrared, or white LEDs, with equal likelihood.
source: The sequence of sessions for each rat was randomized, with each day's session illuminated by either red, infrared, or white LEDs, with equal likelihood.
evidence: This is a direct statement about the experimental design.

claim: In unbiased rats, the point of subjective equality (PSE) should be at 45°.
source: In unbiased rats, the PSE should be at 45˚.
evidence: This is based on the task design where 45° is the boundary between the two categories.
